title: Explainer: absolute vs relative risk + NNT/NNH
context: One of the most consequential statistical misreadings in oncology is conflating relative risk reduction with absolute benefit. A '30% reduction in risk of death' sounds dramatic but may represent an absolute difference of only 2-3 percentage points if baseline risk is low, translating to a number-needed-to-treat of 33-50 patients — a very different clinical picture. This misreading affects how clinicians, patients, and payers perceive drug benefit and toxicity trade-offs. This explainer is part of the M1 core set and depends on template-002 and repro-005.
objective: Author, biostatistician-sign-off, and publish the absolute vs relative risk and NNT/NNH explainer — covering the definitions, the relationship between them, the classic relative-vs-absolute misreading trap, and the correct interpretation of NNT and NNH — with a reproducible worked example on open/synthetic data.
acceptanceCriteria: The explainer provides plain and precise definitions of absolute risk, relative risk, relative risk reduction, absolute risk reduction, number-needed-to-treat (NNT), and number-needed-to-harm (NNH) with their mathematical relationships stated.

An explicit commonTrap section corrects the classic misreading: a large relative risk reduction does not imply a large absolute benefit; the baseline risk determines how much absolute benefit the relative reduction translates to. Both the misleading presentation (relative only) and the correct interpretation (relative + absolute + NNT) are illustrated.
NNH is introduced alongside NNT so clinicians can evaluate benefit vs harm trade-offs rather than benefit alone.
A worked example on open/synthetic data has a manifest entry; all figures and numbers emitted by committed script and diff-matched by repro-005.
Every conceptual claim cites an authoritative methods source; howToReadInAPaper checklist included.
Structural 'educational, not clinical advice' framing present; the explainer does not advise whether any specific benefit/harm trade-off is worth it.
Biostatistician statsSignoff recorded; clinical-framing and accessibility reviews passed.
Zero stated statistical falsehoods; pnpm build && pnpm test && pnpm lint pass; DCO signed-off.
resources: Reporting guidelines: CONSORT, SAMPL; EQUATOR network
Methods literature on NNT, absolute vs relative risk presentation (cited per-claim)
output: A published, biostatistician-signed-off absolute vs relative risk and NNT/NNH explainer with 100% reproducible worked example, full provenance, all claims cited, accessibility passed, and structural 'not clinical advice' frame.
